item.title: San Giorgio Maggiore at Dusk
item.lesson: End where the light ends. Across the water from San Marco, the island church of San Giorgio Maggiore — the same silhouette Whistler found in the dark thirty years earlier — stands against a sky Monet has set entirely on fire. This is the most famous canvas of his Venice autumn: the campanile and dome burned to a blue-violet shadow, the lagoon running molten orange and rose, the sun already gone and everything still blazing with its memory. It is the exhibition's question answered in the affirmative, at last and completely — light on water not only painted but painted as the entire visible world, architecture reduced to the small dark thing the light happens around. Monet finished it as he finished all the Venice pictures: at home in Giverny, eyes closed over the canvas, painting what remained of Venice in his mind. A century later the Venice canvases have scattered to museums on four continents, and reunions are rare. One is happening now — until July 26, they hang together again at the de Young, in Golden Gate Park.
item.lookFor: Watch how the water's brushstrokes change direction — horizontal calm at the edges, then rippling diagonals where the fire pours across the middle. The reflection is more alive than the sky it reflects.
